coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots coupled with morin-Al3+: Cleverly design an integrated sensing platform for ratiometric optical dual-mode and smartphone-assisted visual detection of fluoride ion
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Yumin
Dong, Wenjuan
Liu, Yang
Wen, Xiaole
Shuang, Shaomin
Hu, Qin
Dong, Chuan
Gong, Xiaojuan
coredata.dc:description: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor has high selectivity and good sensitivity; however, its development is limited by intricate design, tedious synthesis, etc. Herein, a facile and effective ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform for fluoride ion (F-) detection was developed by simply combining nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and morin-Al3+ based on inner filter effect (IFE). The competitive binding of F- to Al3+ obviously decreased morin-Al3+ fluorescence and increased N-CDs fluorescence, attributing to the inhibition of IFE between N-CDs and morin-Al3+. The as-constructed ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform can be used for F- detection with a wide linear range (0.5–150 μM) and a low detection limit (55.8 nM). Interestingly, with the introduction of F- into the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ sensing platform, a distinguishable change in fluorescence color from green to blue enabled the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system to be used as a smartphone-assisted visual sensing platform for F- detection with a detection limit of 2.09 μM. This platform was successfully applied for the onsite monitoring of F- in various water samples with satisfying results. These findings provide a novel guidance for the facile construction of a ratiometric optical dual-mode and smartphone-assisted sensing platform based on CDs, revealing the broad application prospect of CDs in environmental monitoring field.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots coupled with morin-Al3+: Cleverly design an integrated sensing platform for ratiometric optical dual-mode and smartphone-assisted visual detection of fluoride ion

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor has high selectivity and good sensitivity; however, its development is limited by intricate design, tedious synthesis, etc. Herein, a facile and effective ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform for fluoride ion (F-) detection was developed by simply combining nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and morin-Al3+ based on inner filter effect (IFE). The competitive binding of F- to Al3+ obviously decreased morin-Al3+ fluorescence and increased N-CDs fluorescence, attributing to the inhibition of IFE between N-CDs and morin-Al3+. The as-constructed ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform can be used for F- detection with a wide linear range (0.5–150 μM) and a low detection limit (55.8 nM). Interestingly, with the introduction of F- into the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ sensing platform, a distinguishable change in fluorescence color from green to blue enabled the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system to be used as a smartphone-assisted visual sensing platform for F- detection with a detection limit of 2.09 μM. This platform was successfully applied for the onsite monitoring of F- in various water samples with satisfying results. These findings provide a novel guidance for the facile construction of a ratiometric optical dual-mode and smartphone-assisted sensing platform based on CDs, revealing the broad application prospect of CDs in environmental monitoring field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluoride ion (F-) is widely found in drinking water, toothpaste and osteoporosis drugs (Chae et al., 2016), which has attracted widespread attention due to its potential health risk (Hudnall et al., 2009). Proper intake of F- is essential for maintaining healthy teeth and skeleton (Mandal et al., 2016); however, excessive intake of F- can damage human organs, such as stomach, kidney and bones (Singhal et al., 2017; Ahmadijokani et al., 2021). Moreover, long-term exposure to a high level of F- can negatively affect the children's IQ (Yu et al., 2018). More importantly, the groundwater in many regions of the earth contains a plenty of F-, causing inevitable threats and serious side effects to human beings (Reddy et al., 2010). Therefore, World Health Organization (WHO) and China have strictly stipulated that the maximum permitted levels of F- in drinking water should not excess 1.5 mg·L−1 (79.0 µM) and 1.0 mg·L−1 (52.6 µM), respectively (Tian et al., 2021). Hence, it is of great significance to develop a facile, sensitive and selective method for F- detection. Some commercial methods including ion-chromatography (Marocco et al., 2021), ion-selective electrode (Klssa, 1987) and colorimetric methods (Bhat et al., 2016) have been adopted to detect F-. However, these methods were difficult to achieve sensitive detection of F- and had poor reproducibility. Recently, the fluorescence method was regarded as the most promising method for F- detection due to its advantages of low cost, superior sensitivity and easy visualization (Guo et al., 2017).

So far, most of the fluorescence sensors for F- detection were constructed based on the single emission wavelength, which is inevitably affected by environmental factors and instrument efficiency (Park et al., 2020). The ratiometric fluorescence sensors can effectively eliminate these adverse distractions and greatly improve the reliability of analytical data, attributing to its intrinsic self-calibration (Han et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2021). More importantly, the apparent color changes of the ratiometric fluorescence sensors can be easily distinguished by the naked eye, endowing the sensors with excellent visualization capability (Castro et al., 2021). Theoretically, the ratiometric fluorescence sensors for F- detection can be constructed by individual or combined fluorophores. For instance, Zhang and Zhang (2021) synthesized a UiO-66-NH2 @eosin Y composite by confining eosin Y into the cavities of Zr-MOF, which can act as a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the detection of F-. Yang et al. (2017) reported a boric acid functionalized lanthanide metal-organic frameworks (LMOFs) for the ratiometric detection of F-. Shen et al. (2018) has constructed a ratiometric fluorescence F- detection system based on diethylamino-coumarin derivative through an ICT-modulated strategy. Dhanunjayarao et al. (2016) prepared an imidazole-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor for F- detection by the selective cleavage of Si-O bond. However, most of these ratiometric fluorescence sensors were constructed by covalent bonding or surface modification, which made their designing and construction process complicated and was difficult to be further popularized in many fields. Consequently, it is of great significance to select a suitable material to construct a ratiometric fluorescence sensor with low-cost and easy-preparation for F- detection.

Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging class of fluorescent nanomaterials with superior optical properties, small size effects, low cost and easy preparation (Zhang and Yu, 2016; Xu et al., 2020; Sun and Lei, 2017), has become a promising candidate for the construction of fluorescence sensors (Han et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2021). Several CDs-based fluorescence sensors have been reported for the detection of F-. Raveendran et al. (2022) directly detected F- by using arrowroot derived CDs and obtained a detection limit of 521 nM. Liu et al. (2013) and Mohapatra et al. (2015) designed Zr(CDs-COO)2EDTA and Fe3O4@SiO2-EDTA-Ni@CD, achieving a detection limit of 0.031 µM F- and 0.06 µM F-, respectively. Singhal et al. (2017) and Boruah et al. (2020) constructed the on-off-on fluorescence probes to detect F- based on the quenching effect of Eu3+ on CDs fluorescence and the fluorescence recovering ability of F- towards the Eu3+-CDs system. These probes can realize the detection of F- based on the change of single-wavelength fluorescence intensity, and their sensitivity could be easily affected by the interference of detection environment and background. Fortunately, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor which has self-calibration function can effectively eliminate the influence of temperature, concentration, environmental pH, polarity, etc., and found their significant place in sensing applications due to its high sensitivity and favorable accuracy (Han et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2021). By connecting pyrene-boronic acid and CDs, Li et al. (2020) designed a ratiometric sensing platform for F- detection. Although it achieved good detection results, the preparation and purification steps of this method were quite complicated. In addition, the visual sensing platform can detect the target object by observing the color change with the naked eye, which has the advantages of intuition, simplicity and convenience, and is expected to make the detection more miniaturized, portable and intelligent. Hence, it is necessary to construct a ratiometric fluorescence and smartphone-assisted visual sensing platform based on CDs with great simplicity, good selectivity, high sensitivity, excellent specificity and visual detection ability for F- detection.

In this work, a ratiometric fluorescence and smartphone-assisted visual sensing platform was constructed by simply combining nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and morin-Al3+. In the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system, N-CDs and morin-Al3+ served as reference signal and response signal, respectively. The morin-Al3+ can quench the fluorescence of N-CDs based on inner filter effect (IFE). F- can competitively combine Al3+ of morin-Al3+ and weaken the fluorescence of morin-Al3+. F- can constrain IFE to recover the fluorescence of N-CDs with a marked change in fluorescence color from green to blue and therefore, F- can be detected by the ratiometric fluorescence and smartphone-assisted visual sensing platform ( Scheme 1). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that a ratiometric optical dual-mode sensor constructed by CDs and metal complexes was used for F- detection. For the first time, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on CDs with significant color changes was converted to a smartphone-assisted sensing platform for the detection of F-. Moreover, the proposed method was successfully applied for the detection of F- in water samples with satisfying results, providing the possibility for the application in environmental monitoring. Our work provides a novel idea for the controllable designing of ratiometric sensors and realizing portable and handheld detection with the assistance of smartphone, contributing to the rapid development of CDs-based sensing field.

o-Phenylenediamine (o-PDA), citric acid (CA), morin, sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), glacial acetic acid (CH3COOH), sodium acetate (CH3COONa), lanthanum nitrate (LaNO3.6 H2O), alizarin complexone and acetone were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cationic solutions of Na+, K+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Cr3+, Ni2+ and Al3+ were prepared from their respective chloride or nitrate salts. Anionic solutions of NO2 -, NO3 -, SO3 2-, SO4 2-, CH3COO-, ClO3 -, ClO4 -, SCN-, S2-, Cl-, Br-, I- and F- were formulated by their respective sodium or potassium salts. All chemicals were of analytical grade or above and used as received without further purification. Ultrapure water (≥ 18.25 MΩ·cm) was applied throughout the experiments.

The morphologies of N-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). The surface structures and elemental constituents of N-CDs were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray Diffraction (XRD) and elemental analysis. The optical features of N-CDs and the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system were measured by UV–vis absorption, photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra. In addition, fluorescence lifetime was implemented to investigate the sensing mechanism. The correlative apparatus information and parameters were referred to the previously published articles (Gong et al., 2015, 2016). All photos were taken by a smartphone with the model of HUAWEI Nova 8.

The N-CDs were favorably obtained via a one-step hydrothermal carbonization method. Briefly, o-PDA (108 mg) and CA (192 mg) were dissolved in ultrapure water (10 mL) and subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. The prepared solution was sealed in a Teflon autoclave (50 mL) to heat at 120 °C for 6 h, and then the autoclave was cooled naturally in air to room temperature. The obtained dark-red solution was purified in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 100–500 Da against ultrapure water for 12 h by recharging ultrapure water every 6 h. Then, the processed solution was filtered through the filter membrane (0.22 μm). Finally, the dark-orange powders were collected by freeze-drying for further use.

In a typical procedure, 5.0 μL N-CDs solution (10 mg·mL−1), 2.5 μL morin solution (0.01 M in ethanol) and 10 μL Al3+ solution (0.01 M) were successively added into 2.0 mL ultrapure water. Then, the mixture was treated by ultra-sonication for 5 min to prepare the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of relative fluorescence quantum yield of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:

where Grad and η are the gradient of the integrated PL intensity against absorbance and the index of refractive, respectively. The subscripts “ST” and “X” represent the standard reference and N-CDs, respectively. The quinine sulfate (dissolved 0.1 M H2SO4) with a FQY of 0.54 under the excitation wavelength of 375 nm was selected as the standard reference. η
X
/η
ST is approximately 1.0.

Briefly, 2.0 μL of F- solution with a certain concentration was introduced into the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system, and then the FL and UV–vis absorption spectra were captured in detail. The obtained PL intensities (F432 nm/F492 nm) at the excitation wavelength of 375 nm and the absorbances (A355 nm/A415 nm) were used to construct the ratiometric optical dual-mode sensor for F- detection and to calculate the linear range of the proposed sensor. In order to ensure the stability of the light source when taking photos, we chose to add a 365 nm UV lamp in the darkroom for the simulation of ultraviolet light. And a fixed angle and position was selected for taking photos to possibly ensure the quality of the photos and minimize background interference. Under the pre-set ultraviolet light irradiation, the fluorescence photos of the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system with different concentrations of F- were captured by a smartphone camera. Then, the obtained photos were analyzed by the applet (Color Identification) installed on a smartphone. All measurements were conducted in triplicate (n = 3).

The sensitivity and accuracy of the smartphone-assisted sensing platform for F- detection in the presence of the other contaminants was further studied in detail. Firstly, 60.0 µM F- was added into the solution (the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system) containing various contaminants, including Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, SO4 2-, Cl-, I-, Br- and NO3 - at a concentration of 0.5 mM. And then the fluorescence photos of the solution were captured by a smartphone camera and analyzed by the applet (Color Identification) installed on a smartphone. In addition, the spiked recovery and relative standard deviations (RSDs) of F- were calculated. All measurements were conducted in triplicate (n = 3).

Tap water, surface water (Fenhe river, Shanxi, China) and simulated groundwater were utilized to evaluate the feasibility of the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system by introducing various concentrations of F- (5 μM, 10 μM, 20 μM or 60 μM). Simulated groundwater was composed of Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, SO4 2- and Cl- at a concentration of 1.0 mM and I-, Br- and NO3 - with a concentration of 0.5 mM. The water samples were measured by the proposed ratiometric fluorescence and smartphone-assisted visual sensor based on the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system, the specific operations were as follows. 5.0 μL N-CDs solution (10 mg·mL−1), 2.5 μL morin solution (0.01 M in ethanol) and 10 μL Al3+ solution (0.01 M) were successively added into 1.0 mL water sample and treated by ultra-sonication for 5 min to prepare the mixture solution. Subsequently, the mixture solution was introduced into 1.0 mL water sample with the known concentrations of F-, and then the PL and UV–vis absorption spectra were estimated in detail. Further, the fluorescence photos were captured by a smartphone camera and analyzed by the applet (Color Identification) installed on a smartphone.

The fluorine-spectrophotometry (HJ 488–2009) was used to detect F- in real samples. Firstly, 0.0443 g of LaNO3.6 H2O was dissolved in a small number of HCl solution (1.0 M). And then pH of the above solution was adjusted to 4.1 by CH3COONa solution (1.0 M), which was further diluted with ultrapure water to 100 mL for preparing LaNO3 solution with the concentration of 0.001 M. Secondly, 0.0193 g of alizarin complexone and 0.0125 g CH3COONa was dissolved in a small quantity of NaOH solution (1.0 M). And then pH of the above solution was adjusted to 5.0 by HCl solution (1.0 M), which was further diluted with ultrapure water to 50 mL for obtaining alizarin complexone solution (0.001 M). Thirdly, CH3COOH-CH3COONa buffer solution with pH 4.1 was prepared for further use. Fourthly, 3.0 mL alizarin complexone solution (0.001 M), 1.0 mL buffer solution (pH 4.1), 3.0 mL acetone and 3.0 mL LaNO3 solution (0.001 M) were added into the beaker to form the chromogenic reagent. Notably, the chromogenic reagent should be fresh and used instantly. Fifthly, 1.0 mL of the standard F- solution with different concentrations was mixed with 1.0 mL of the chromogenic reagent for the chromogenic reaction of 30 min. The corresponding absorbances at 572 nm were measured with ultrapure water as reference, and then the standard curve was obtained by plotting absorbances with F- concentrations. Finally, the standard F- solution was replaced by the actual water sample solution spiked with known concentrations of F-, following the same procedure as mentioned above for the detection of F- content.

Morphology and dimension of N-CDs were characterized by the typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As displayed in Fig. 1A, N-CDs were nearly spherical and uniformly dispersed on the copper grid with a size range of 1.8–4.0 nm and an average diameter of 2.77 nm. As shown by the high-resolution TEM image (the inserted figure in Fig. 1A), the lattice spacing of N-CDs was 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) planes of graphite carbon, indicating that N-CDs had highly crystalline carbon structures (Tang et al., 2014). The XRD pattern of N-CDs exhibited a peak at around 20° (Fig. S1), which is related to the graphitic structure with interlayer spacing (002) of 0.45 nm (Guo et al., 2022). Compared with 0.335 nm of graphite, the enlarged interlayer distance of N-CDs may be attributed to the plan distortion from sp3 C in the sp2-carbon domain or the steric hindrance of functional groups on the sp2-carbon plan edge (Guo et al., 2022). The results of TEM and XRD revealed that N-CDs had nanocrystalline cores of graphitic sp2 carbon atoms along with sp3 carbon defects.

Surface structure and elemental composition of N-CDs were explored by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and elemental analysis. The FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 1B) exhibited many hydrophilic groups such as CO (1630 cm−1), C-O (1000–1150 cm−1) and O-H/N-H (3432 cm−1) on the surface of N-CDs, endowing N-CDs with good water solubility (Chao et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020). Some peaks at 1630 cm−1, 1584 cm−1 and 1225 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CO, CC and C-N, respectively, indicating the presence of polyaromatic structures on the surface of N-CDs (Vallan et al., 2018). In addition, the aliphatic C-H stretching vibration (2800–3024 cm−1) and in-plane O-H vibration (1395 cm−1) can be observed in the FT-IR spectrum (Wang et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2021a, 2021b). By comparing the FT-IR spectra of N-CDs (blue line in Fig. S2) with that of the reaction precursors CA (black line in Fig. S2) and o-PDA (red line in Fig. S2), some remarkable changes can be found, including the disappearance of two peaks around 3300 cm−1 (-NH2 stretching vibration in the FT-IR spectrum of o-PDA) and the shift of the peak from 1743 cm−1 (-COOH stretching vibration in the FT-IR spectrum of CA) to 1702 cm−1 (-CONH stretching vibration in the FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs), illustrating that the dehydration, condensation and carbonization of -NH2 groups in o-PDA and -COOH groups in CA have probably experienced during the hydrothermal treatment process to form N-CDs (Shen at al, 2021; Zhang et al., 2021). The elemental and structural compositions of N-CDs were further confirmed by XPS and elemental analysis. As demonstrated in Fig. 1C, the full XPS spectrum of N-CDs contains three typical peaks at 282.0 eV, 398.0 eV and 529.0 eV, respectively corresponding to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, which suggests that N-CDs were primarily composed of C, N and O. Meanwhile, the elemental analysis was used to obtain the corresponding element contents of C, H, N and O with the results of 51.39%, 5.13%, 10.02% and 33.46% (calculated), respectively, which is depicted in Table S1. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 1D) illustrated four constituent peaks at 288.9 eV, 287.6 eV, 286.0 eV and 284.8 eV, which are ascribed to O-CO, CO, C-O/C-N and CC/C-C, respectively (Wang et al., 2020, 2021). The N 1s band (Fig. 1E) had two constituent peaks, corresponding to graphitic N (400.9 eV) and pyridinic N (398.9 eV) (Chao et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020). The two-constituent peaks of O 1s band (Fig. 1F) at 532.7 eV and 531.5 eV were attributed to C-O and CO, respectively (Wang et al., 2020; Bogireddy et al., 2020). The analysis results of XPS are completely consistent with those of FT-IR, demonstrating that the abundant functional groups including C-O, C-N, CC, etc. can be found on the surface of N-CDs.

Optical features of N-CDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption, photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs (black line in Fig. 2A) showed two clear pinnacles at 269 nm and 277 nm, which are attributed to the π→π∗ transition of the aromatic sp2 domains (Liu et al., 2017). Also, a faint and broad peak at 450 nm corresponding to the n→π∗ transition of the surface groups (Jiao et al., 2019) can be observed in the lower-energy region. N-CDs displayed excellent solubility in ultrapure water to form a clear light-yellow solution and can emit intense blue fluorescence (the inserted photos in Fig. 2A). Interestingly, the solid powder of N-CDs was in orange color under the irradiation of visible light and can emit bright red solid fluorescence under the irradiation of ultraviolet light (insert of Fig. 2A), which may be attributed to the polymer structure of N-CDs. This specific structure not only effectively inhibits the π-π interaction of carbon nucleus, but also avoids the direct contact of surface sub-fluorophore (Gao et al., 2021a, 2021b). Under the optimal excitation wavelength of 375 nm (purple line in Fig. 2A), N-CDs can exhibit narrow blue emission band at 432 nm (blue line in Fig. 2A) with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of ~67 nm. Moreover, as the excitation wavelength of N-CDs red-shifted from 325 nm to 425 nm, the PL emission peaks of N-CDs remained unchanged at ~432 nm (Fig. 2B), indicating that N-CDs had the excitation-independent PL behavior, which is closely related to the uniform surface structure of N-CDs (Pan et al., 2015). Using quinine sulfate as the standard reference (Fig. S3A), the relative fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) of N-CDs in ultrapure water was calculated to be 42.18% (Fig. S3B). The high FQY of N-CDs may be attributed to the doping of electron-rich nitrogen atoms in N-CDs (Miao et al., 2020).

The PL stabilities of N-CDs under various environments are crucial for its multiple applications (Sun et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2020). Firstly, the relative PL intensities of N-CDs under phosphate buffer saline (PBS) with different pHs were obtained and the result is shown in Fig. S4A. Except for extremely acidic (pH < 4.0) and alkaline (pH > 9.0) environments, the relative PL intensities of N-CDs were between 0.92 and 1.01 in a near-neutral pH range of 4.0–9.0, indicating that N-CDs are not susceptible to pH fluctuations and had good PL stability under physiological environments. As shown by Fig. S4B, the N-CDs can still maintain PL stability under high concentration of KCl solution (4.0 M), indicating that N-CDs had excellent salt-resistance. The effect of temperature on the PL intensity of N-CDs was studied and the result is displayed in Fig. S4C. The relative PL intensity of N-CDs did not change within the tested temperature range of 4–65oC, indicating that N-CDs can be applied in both low temperature and high temperature environment. As shown in Fig. S4D, the PL intensity of N-CDs was not affected by the ultra-high concentration of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 1.0 M), illustrating the high anti-oxidant ability of N-CDs. To sum up, the PL intensities of N-CDs were not affected by pH, ion, temperature and oxidizing substances, and its good stability lays a foundation for further application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design principle of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The ratiometric sensor for F- detection was constructed by integrating the blue emissive N-CDs and green emissive morin-Al3+. Morin-Al3+, as the efficient energy transfer system based on the chelation between morin and Al3+, emit a strong green fluorescence (Wang et al., 2005). Based on the competition coordination, morin-Al3+ can be used for the single-wavelength fluorescence detection of F-. The detection results may be affected by background or detection environment, resulting in decreased sensitivity and accuracy (Park et al., 2020). The ratiometric fluorescence sensor can effectively overcome the interference from background and detection environment by its intrinsic self-calibration function with good selectivity, high sensitivity, excellent anti-interference performance and reliable detection results (Han et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2021). In this work, N-CDs and morin-Al3+ were selected as luminous candidates to establish the ratiometric sensor with the following design principle. Firstly, N-CDs and morin-Al3+ were easily obtained to construct the ratiometric sensor, which is convenient and fast without any covalent bonding and surface modification. The immobilization of N-CDs and morin-Al3+ was investigated by zeta potential measurement, UV–vis absorption spectra and PL spectra. As shown in Fig. S5, the ζ-potential of N-CDs, morin and N-CDs/morin are + 2.94 mV, −9.56 mV and −5.95 mV, respectively, demonstrating a strong electrostatic interaction occurred between N-CDs and morin. Meanwhile, the chelation between morin and Al3+ was further verified by PL and UV–vis absorption spectra. With the continuous addition of Al3+ into morin, the PL intensity of morin was significantly enhanced (Fig. S6A), and the absorption peak of morin changed from 375 nm to 415 nm (Fig. S6B), indicating the formation of a new morin-Al3+. However, Al3+ has no effect on PL and absorption spectra of N-CDs (Fig. S6C and S6D), manifesting no interaction between Al3+ and N-CDs. When Al3+ was introduced into N-CDs/morin, the ζ-potential of N-CDs/morin was changed from −5.95 mV to 0.71 mV (Fig. S5), which suggests that the electrostatic interaction can be found between N-CDs/morin and Al3+. In addition, with the continuous addition of Al3+ into N-CDs/morin, the PL intensity and absorbance of N-CDs/morin at 432 nm and 355 nm gradually decreased (Fig. S6E and S6F), while the PL intensity and absorbance of N-CDs/morin at 492 nm and 415 nm gradually increased (Fig. S6E and S6F), respectively, demonstrating that Al3+ can combine with N-CDs/morin to form a new N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system by chelation. Therefore, morin can effectively combine to the surface of N-CDs by electrostatic interaction to form N-CDs/morin, and then Al3+ can efficiently chelate with morin and electrically adsorb with N-CDs/morin to construct the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system.

Secondly, the fluorescence spectrum of the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system showed two distinct emission peaks under the same excitation wavelength. As displayed in Fig. 3A and 3B, N-CDs and morin-Al3+ showed the excitation-independent emission behavior, ensuring that the dual emission signal of the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system can be steadily obtained under the optimal excitation wavelength of 375 nm. To verify the possibility of dual emission wavelength in the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system, the normalized PL spectra of N-CDs, morin-Al3+ and N-CDs/morin-Al3+ were investigated and the results are displayed in Fig. 3C. Under the excitation wavelength of 375 nm, the emission wavelengths of N-CDs, morin-Al3+ and the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system were located at 432 nm, 504 nm and 432 and 492 nm, respectively, demonstrating that the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system can show two distinct emission peaks, which would be possible to construct a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system.

Thirdly, the interaction between Al3+ and F- was stronger than that between Al3+ and morin, which lays a solid foundation for constructing a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system for F- detection. As depicted in Figs. 3D and 3E, with the addition of F-, the PL intensity of morin-Al3+ was dramatically decreased, and the absorption peak of morin-Al3+ changed from 415 nm to 387 nm, which indicates that Al3+ was stripped from morin-Al3+ by F- to form a more stable Al3+-F- complex with F-. Based on this information, morin-Al3+ can selectively respond to F- without interference and quench the fluorescence of morin-Al3+ at 504 nm (Fig. S7A). Meanwhile, the PL intensity of N-CDs at 432 nm was not affected by other ions (Fig. S7B) or different concentrations of F- (Fig. S7C), demonstrating that N-CDs can be used as reference signal. Furthermore, when F- was introduced into the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system, the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system showed two obvious emission peaks at 432 nm (N-CDs) and 492 nm (morin-Al3+), respectively (Fig. 3F). Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system can be constructed for the specific detection of F-. All in all, the construction of a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for F- detection based on the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system was mainly attributed to the following design principles, including the easy preparation of two luminaries without modification, two luminaries with the same excitation wavelength and different emission wavelengths and the opposite fluorescence trends of two luminaries (one increase and one decrease) after the introduction of F- into the system. These design concepts provide a solid theoretical basis for the construction of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on two luminaries, which is helpful to enrich the construction strategy of the ratiometric fluorescence sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric sensor for the detection of F -
ce:section.ce:para: The PL stability of the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system is a primary condition to ensure that it can be used as a fluorescence sensor. The PL spectra of the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system were obtained under different concentrations of salt solution, UV irradiation time and diverse temperatures, and the results are depicted in Fig. S8. The PL intensity of the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system hardly changed under all treatment conditions, including 0.2–1.0 M KCl (Fig. S8A), 0–60 min UV irradiation (Figs. S8B) and 5–55oC temperature environments (Fig. S8C), indicating that this system had outstanding fluorescence stability and can resist high salt, strong light irradiation and high temperature. As an important parameter, the sensitivity of the ratiometric sensor can be improved by optimizing the reaction pH, the molar ratio of morin and Al3+ and the reaction time. As depicted in Fig. 4A, the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system without and with F- showed a consistent PL intensity ratio (F432 nm/F492 nm) at pH 6.5–8.0 and in ultrapure water. To eliminate the buffer preparation process, the ultrapure water as neutral medium was selected as the optimal solvent for F- detection. The optimum concentrations of N-CDs and morin were firstly determined. When the concentration of Al3+ was fixed at 50.0 µM, the PL spectra of the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system formed by different concentrations of N-CDs and morin were obtained and are shown in Fig. S9. When the concentrations of N-CDs and morin were 0.050 mg·mL−1 and 50.0 µM (Fig. S9A), 0.025 mg·mL−1 and 50.0 µM (Fig. S9B), 0.050 mg·mL−1 and 25.0 µM (Fig. S9C) and 0.025 mg·mL−1 and 25.0 µM (Fig. S9D), respectively, the PL intensities of these N-CDs/morin-Al3+ systems exceed the upper limit of the instrument and do not meet the test requirement. The N-CDs/morin-Al3+ systems had good spectral characteristics when the concentrations of N-CDs and morin were 0.050 mg·mL−1 and 12.5 µM (Fig. S9E) and 0.025 mg·mL−1 and 12.5 µM (Fig. S9F), respectively. Considering that the greater the ratio of the PL intensity of the two emission peaks in the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system, the higher the response sensitivity to F-, the optimal concentrations of N-CDs and morin were determined as 0.025 mg·mL−1 and 12.5 µM, respectively. In addition, the molar ratio of morin and Al3+ will directly affect the sensitivity of the constructed sensor for F- detection. When the concentrations of N-CDs and morin were 0.025 mg·mL−1 and 12.5 μM, respectively, the F432 nm/F492 nm under different molar ratios of Al3+/morin were obtained and the results are shown in Fig. 4B. The difference value (∆F432 nm/F492 nm) of N-CDs/morin-Al3+ + F- (F432 nm/F492 nm) and N-CDs/morin-Al3+ (F432 nm/F492 nm) was increased and then decreased with increasing the molar ratio of Al3+/morin (the inserted figure in Fig. 4B). When the molar ratio of Al3+/morin was 4.0, the ∆F432 nm/F492 nm reached its maximum value and therefore, 4.0 was selected as the optimal molar ratio of Al3+/morin. The influence of response time on F- detection was also studied. As displayed in Fig. 4C, when the reaction time reached or exceeded 60 s, the detection of F- based on the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system can achieve equilibrium. Therefore, 1.0 min was considered as optimal response time. All in all, ultrapure water, a molar ratio of Al3+/morin at 4.0 and reaction time of 1.0 min were used as the optimum conditions for F- detection.

Under the optimal conditions, various concentrations of F- were introduced into the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system and the PL intensities were recorded. As displayed in Fig. 5 A, with the increase of F- concentration in the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system, the PL intensities at 492 nm decreased regularly accompanying with a recovered emission band at 432 nm. An exact linear relationship can be found between the ratio of PL intensity (F432 nm/F492 nm) and the concentration of F- in a range of 0.5–150 µM with a linear regression equation and correlation coefficient (R2) of F432 nm/F492 nm = 0.0092 C + 0.3574 and 0.998 (Fig. 5B), respectively, where C is the concentration of F-. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 55.8 nM (S/N = 3), which was lower than the maximum allowable detection level of F- (1.0 mg·L−1, equal to 52.6 µM) in drinking water set by Sanitary Standards for Drinking Water in China [8]. The addition of F- into the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system not only had a great influence on the PL intensity of the system, but also had a significant effect on the UV–vis absorption spectra of the system. The absorbance of the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system at 355 nm was gradually enhanced and that at 415 nm was markedly weakened when the different concentrations of F- were added into the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system (Fig. 5 C). As depicted in Fig. 5D, the absorbance ratio (A355 nm/A415 nm) and F- concentration had a good linear relationship in a range of 0.5–65 µM with the fitting equation and LOD of A355 nm/A415 nm = 0.0173 C + 0.6239 (R2 = 0.998) and 84.2 nM (S/N = 3), respectively, where C is F- concentration. Further, the selectivity and anti-interference are important indexes for evaluating the performance of the constructed sensor, which was handled by introducing various coexisting ions (Na+, K+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Cr3+, Ni2+, NO2 -, NO3 -, SO3 2-, SO4 2-, CH3COO-, ClO3 -, ClO4 -, SCN-, S2-, Cl-, Br- and I-) at a concentration of 750 µM in the absence and presence of F- (150 µM), respectively. As shown in Figs. 5E and 5 F, only F- had obvious influence on F432 nm/F492 nm and A355 nm/A415 nm, indicating that the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system has good selectivity for F- detection. When other interfering ions exist in the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system, F- still had a significant effect on F432 nm/F492 nm and A355 nm/A415 nm, illustrating that the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system had a high anti-interference performance for F- detection. These results demonstrated that the ratiometric optical sensor based on the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system for detecting F- had a high grade of sensitivity, selectivity and anti-interference performance.

Another interesting discovery was that the fluorescence color of the N-CDs/mroin-Al3+ system under UV light (365 nm) was changed significantly from green to blue after the introduction of different concentrations of F- into the N-CDs/mroin-Al3+ system ( Fig. 6 A), endowing the N-CDs/mroin-Al3+ system with the ability for visually detecting F-. This obvious color change of green (2.5 μM F-) and blue (150 μM F-) was further recorded by CIE coordinates with the results of (0.1801, 0.3730) and (0.1566, 0.1484) (Fig. 6B), and the color change calibration curve displayed a correlation coefficient (R2) as high as 0.992 (the inserted figure in Fig. 6B), demonstrating that the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system has the great potential to build a visual sensing platform for precise detection of F-. We choose a smartphone-assisted strategy to improve the accuracy, sensitivity and applicability of visual detection of F- with the specific implementation steps in Fig. 6 A. Firstly, the fluorescence photos of the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system with different concentrations of F- were instantly captured by a smartphone camera (HUAWEI Nova 8). Secondly, the obtained photos were recognized by the Color Identification applet to transform into the corresponding RGB values, where R, G and B represent red, green and blue, respectively. Finally, in order to further ensure the sensitivity and accuracy of the smartphone-assisted visual method, the ratio of B and G (B/G) as the ordinate and F- concentration as the abscissa to establish the linear relationship for the quantitative detection of F-. B/G can minimize the interference from external environment during photo-shooting process. As shown in Fig. 6 C, the B/G and F- concentration displayed a good linear relationship in a range of 2.5–150 μM with a linear regression equation and LOD of B/G = 0.0044 C + 0.9904 (R2 = 0.992) and 2.09 μM (S/N = 3), where C is the concentration of F-. The above results fully illustrated the designed smartphone-assisted sensing platform based on the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system can realize the visual detection of F- with rapid speed and high sensitivity, significantly improving the efficiency of F- detection.

A comparison between the constructed method and the reported analytical methods for the ratiometric detection of F- was shown in Table 1. Compared with the other reported ratiometric methods for F- detection, the as-constructed dual-mode (fluorimetry and colorimetry) ratiometric sensing method not only had wider linear range and lower detection limit, but also can provide more reliable detection results. More importantly, the sensing platform constructed by combining smartphone-assisted visualization method with dual-mode ratiometric method is helpful to realize in-site and real-time detection of F- with lower interference, faster speed, higher sensitivity, etc.

To understand the sensing mechanism, the interaction between the ratiometric sensor and F- was investigated in detail. As displayed in Fig. 7 A, a large overlapping range can be found between the emission spectrum of N-CDs and the absorption spectrum of morin-Al3+, suggesting that inner filter effect (IFE) may be the main quenching mechanism between N-CDs and morin-Al3+ (Zhang et al., 2019; Li et al., 2021). To confirm this speculation, the transient fluorescence spectra of N-CDs, N-CDs/morin and N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system were measured and the results are depicted in Fig. 7B and Table S2. The average fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs, N-CDs/morin and N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system were 9.21 ns, 9.16 ns and 9.12 ns, respectively. No obvious change in these lifetimes demonstrated the presence of efficient IFE between N-CDs and morin-Al3+. Therefore, the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system with dual-emission wavelengths was constructed based on IFE, using N-CDs and morin-Al3+ as fluorophore and absorber, respectively. With the addition of F-, the PL intensity of the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system at 492 nm was observably weakened and that at 432 nm was gradually enhanced (Fig. 5 A); meanwhile, the absorbance of the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system at 415 nm was markedly weakened and that at 355 nm was gradually enhanced (Fig. 5 C). Furthermore, when Al3+ was introduction into the N-CDs/morin mixture, a new absorption peak at 415 nm can be found (Fig. 7 C), indicating the formation of a new substance (a complex of morin and Al3+). When F- was added into the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system, the absorption peak at 415 nm disappeared (Fig. 7 C), demonstrating that F- can peel Al3+ from the surface of the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system to form the Al3+-F- complex. More importantly, the overlapping range between the emission spectrum of N-CDs and the absorption spectrum of morin-Al3+ was larger than that between the emission spectrum of N-CDs and the absorption spectrum of morin (Fig. 7 A). Accordingly, we infer that the formation of the Al3+-F- complex can break up the existing IFE between N-CDs and morin-Al3+. To further verify this inhibiting effect, the correction formula of IFE was used to assess the possibility of IFE inhibition process. Applying the correction formula adopted by Parker and Barnes, the correction factor (CF) of IFE and corrected fluorescence intensity can be calculated according to the following equation (Panigrahi et al., 2019):

ex,em ) and the suppressed efficiency (E) are displayed in Table S3 and Fig. 7D. The suppressed efficiency of corrected fluorescence of N-CDs was obtained on basis of eliminating IFE from the observed fluorescence. As shown in the inserted figure of Fig. 7D, ∆E gradually decreased with the addition of F- into the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system, illustrating that the IFE between N-CDs and morin-Al3+ was inhibited. Consequently, it is believed that the sensing mechanism of the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system towards F- was due to the reason that the addition of F- intensively weakened the IFE between N-CDs and morin-Al3+ ( Scheme 2). In other words, F- can compete with Al3+ and strip Al3+ from the surface of the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system, weakening the IFE between N-CDs and morin-Al3+, thus causing the change of fluorescence spectrum and realizing F- detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and reliability for real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of the as-constructed sensing platform, the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system was applied for F- detection in various water samples including tap water, surface water (Fenhe river) and simulated groundwater. For comparison, the fluorine reagents spectrophotometry (HJ 488–2009) was used as a standard method for F- detection in actual water samples. The recoveries of the sensor and the standard method were calculated by spiking different concentrations of F- into the water samples. All measurements were performed three times (n = 3). As depicted in Table 2, the recoveries of F- in real samples based on the constructed ratiometric fluorimetry and colorimetry methods were in a range of 92.2–110.0%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were lower than 5.41% (n = 3). Table 3 showed that the recoveries and RSDs of F- in water samples based on the smartphone-assisted sensing platform were calculated as 88.3–114.9% and 4.68–13.06% (n = 3), respectively. Furthermore, the linear equation and linear range of F- were obtained by the fluorine reagents spectrophotometry (Fig. S10), which was applied to investigate the recoveries of F- in actual samples and the results are displayed in Table 3. For the fluorine reagents spectrophotometry, the recoveries and RSDs of F- in water samples were 98.0–103.5% and 1.34–5.18% (n = 3), respectively. The detection results of F- obtained by the as-constructed sensing platform are basically consistent with those obtained by the fluorine reagents spectrophotometry method, demonstrating that the instrument-assisted ratiometric dual-mode and the smartphone-assisted visualization sensing platform based on the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system can be applied for the quantitative detection of F- in actual samples with a high degree of accuracy and reliability.

Considering that there is a certain difference between the detection results of the smartphone-assisted sensing platform (Table 3) and that of the ratiometric optical sensor (Table 2), we further discuss the possible reasons. We made an in-depth analysis of the sensitivity and accuracy of the smartphone-assisted sensing platform for F- detection in the presence of the other contaminants. 60.0 µM F- was added into the solution (the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system) containing various contaminants, including Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, SO4 2-, Cl-, I-, Br- and NO3 -, and then the spiked recovery and RSDs of F- were measured using a smartphone-assisted sensing platform. As depicted in Table S4, the recoveries and RSDs of F- in the solution with and without the other contaminants were 94.8–108.2% and 4.48–9.02% and 94.0% and 7.01%, respectively, indicating that the presence of the other contaminants has little interference on the detection results. Therefore, we infer that the reason for the big difference between the detection result of the smartphone-assisted sensing platform and that of the ratiometric optical dual-mode sensor is that the smartphone-assisted sensing platform itself has certain defects. Although the smartphone-assisted sensing platform can detect target objects in a portable or handheld way, it will inevitably be interfered by the test environment during the detection process, which will cause great fluctuation of the detection results. This is a key problem that needs to be solved for the smartphone-assisted sensing platform. The solution of this problem will help facilitate the rapid development of smartphone-assisted sensing field and promote the popularization and application of smartphone-assisted sensors.

In summary, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with strong blue fluorescence were easily fabricated by a one-step hydrothermal method. Based on IFE, N-CDs and morin-Al3+ can form the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system with dual emission wavelengths. The IFE was weakened by the competitive combination of F- with Al3+ in the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system, resulting in the changes of UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra, which can be used to construct the ratiometric fluorimetry and colorimetry dual-mode sensor for realizing F- detection. Meanwhile, the fluorescence color significantly changed from green to blue with the addition of F- into the N-CDs/morin-Al3+ system, laying a foundation for the construction of a smartphone-assisted sensing platform for the detection of F-. This is the first time that the CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor was used for smartphone-assisted visual detection of F-. More importantly, the as-constructed sensing platform was successfully used for the detection of F- in actual water samples. This work not only provides a new idea for constructing sensors based on CDs, but also broadens the application range of CDs in the field of environmental detection.

Yumin Hao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Software, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Wenjuan Dong: Validation, Methodology. Yang Liu: Software, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Xiaole Wen: Software. Shaomin Shuang: Funding acquisition. Qin Hu: Writing – review & editing. Chuan Dong: Project administration, Supervision. Xiaojuan Gong: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition. All persons who have made substantial contributions to the work reported in the manuscript.

Excessive intake of fluoride ion (F-) can negatively affect some organs and the children's IQ, which is a kind of environmental toxicant that cannot be ignored. Therefore, it is very vital to realize the in-situ detection of F- specifically, accurately, sensitively and rapidly.

The hybrid system (N-CDs/morin-Al3+) of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and morin-Al3+ as a ratiometric optical dual-mode and smartphone-assisted visual sensing platform was used for F- detection. This is the first time that a ratiometric optical dual-mode sensor constructed by CDs and metal complexes with significant color changes was converted to a smartphone-assisted sensing platform for F- detection.
